citation.title: Predictive coding under the free-energy principle
citation.journal: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences

relevance_to_claim: Foundational paper establishing the free-energy / predictive coding framework. Introduces the mathematical formalism in which precision-weighted prediction error (xi = Pi * epsilon) is the unified quantity minimised by the brain. The paper explicitly distinguishes prediction error (epsilon, carried by superficial pyramidal cells ascending the hierarchy) from precision (Pi, a separate parameter controlling post-synaptic gain), and describes two distinct populations: state units encoding predictions and error units encoding prediction errors. Precision weights are not encoded by error unit activity itself but by the synaptic gain applied to error unit outputs. This is the theoretical grounding for the MECH-059 claim that the confidence channel must remain structurally distinct from the residual error signal.
key_quotes: In these generalized schemes, precision is encoded by the synaptic gain (post-synaptic responsiveness) of units reporting prediction errors.
The activities of error units are a function of the states and the dynamics of state units are a function of prediction error.

Sources of forward and backward connections are superficial and deep pyramidal cells respectively. Error units can be identified with superficial pyramidal cells because the only messages that pass up the hierarchy are prediction errors.
notes: The paper does not explicitly frame this as a 'separation requirement' — it treats the architecture as the natural Bayesian formulation. The separation is structural: precision is a parameter of the generative model optimised separately from the state trajectories that produce prediction errors. Conflating them would correspond to treating the inverse-variance as part of the error signal itself, which would break the inference scheme. This implicit separation is exactly what MECH-059 makes explicit as an architectural requirement.
